name: Alocasia 'Loco'
aboutText: Alocasia 'Loco' is a compact, sculptural cultivar derived from tropical Alocasia ancestry of Southeast Asia, selected for its dramatically undulate, puckered leaves with deeply scalloped margins and a bold, glossy dark green surface. Upright petioles arise from a corm-like base, supporting leaves with pronounced pale veins that trace the scalloped outline with architectural precision. The distinctive puckered leaf form — the feature that earns it the name 'Loco' — gives the plant an almost ruffled, animated presence that stands out immediately in any collection.

morphology.leafShape: Ovate with deeply scalloped, undulate margins
morphology.venation: Pronounced pale veins following scalloped outline

propagation.notes: Wait for the mother plant to produce corms or pups before dividing. Larger, well-established plants produce offsets most readily. Alocasias do not propagate reliably from stem cuttings.

careGuide.substrate: Well-draining loamy mix: 40% potting compost, 30% perlite, 20% orchid bark, 10% worm castings. Alocasias need moisture retention unlike most aroids but still require good drainage.
careGuide.watering: Keep evenly moist during the growing season — never waterlogged, never bone dry. Check soil every 3–4 days in summer. Reduce significantly in winter when growth slows.
careGuide.humidity: 60–80%. Lower humidity causes brown leaf edges; very low humidity combined with dry compost will trigger dormancy.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced fertiliser at half strength every 2 weeks during spring and summer. Stop feeding in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Alocasias like being slightly snug in their pots — don't overpot.

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, cold temperatures, or natural dormancy
commonProblems.fix: Reduce watering; ensure temperatures stay above 16°C; dormancy is normal in winter
Increase humidity and maintain consistent watering routine

commonProblems.cause: Most common pest; thrive in hot, dry conditions
commonProblems.fix: Mist regularly; treat with neem oil or insecticidal soap; increase humidity

items: Check the corm is firm — a soft or shrunken corm indicates dehydration or rot and is very difficult to recover
Verify there is at least one established leaf; avoid bare corms from unknown sellers unless you have experience germinating alocasia corms
Spider mites are the most common pest: inspect leaf undersides for fine webbing or stippling, especially in dry indoor environments
Alocasias can enter dormancy when stressed by shipping — a plant arriving with no leaves but a firm corm is not necessarily dead

fieldNotes.title: Going Loco for Texture
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: The name says it all. Alocasia 'Loco' produces leaves with a level of surface drama that most aroids simply don't attempt — the blade is simultaneously scalloped at the margins, puckered across the surface, and traced with bold pale veins that follow every undulation. It reads as compact and architectural but also somehow frenetic in energy, which is presumably the inspiration for the name. Like most cultivated Alocasia it benefits from warm, humid conditions and a well-draining mix, and it rewards consistent care with rapid production of new, equally dramatic leaves.
